text: The higher education system in Africa is going through a period of dynamic change at a historically unprecedented speed. The enrolment in higher education institutions in Africa has increased dramatically over the last decade, leading to what has been called “massification” – a term that reflects the increasing global demand for higher education in a knowledge-based economy and also acknowledges the important role higher education plays in the economic and social development of countries. However, the seemingly unstoppable character of the enrolment growth, which we have likened to a “flash flood”, which sweeps away everything in its path, represents a series of challenges related to the structural makeup of higher education in Africa. In this article we look at some of the developments and challenges in higher education in Africa, focusing on such issues as the relationship between expansion and capacity (human and financial), equity, quality, the increasing importance of internationalisation and quality assurance, and the imbalance between undergraduate and postgraduate education. Our aim is to give an impression of the situation facing higher education in Africa today, by combining recent studies and national reports on developments in higher education in Africa. Our intention is to draw attention to the urgent need to ensure that access to higher education is accompanied by quality, if the higher education sector in Africa is to remain competitive in the years to come. Africa is the fastest-growing higher education sector in the world, but it is also the least developed. Ensuring quality in higher education is a matter of great urgency in Africa.
